Daily DAP Accessibility Report — 2026-03-19#

Run ID: run-2026-03-19-5a0972094f · Status: success · Source data: 2026-03-17 · Generated: 2026-03-19T09:31:17.147Z

About These Reports#

The Digital Analytics Program (DAP) is a U.S. government analytics service that collects website traffic data across participating federal agencies. DAP tracks page views, visitor counts, and usage patterns for hundreds of government websites, providing transparency into how the public engages with federal digital services.

This report measures the quality and accessibility of the top 100 most-visited U.S. government URLs as reported by DAP. Each day, Lighthouse scans are run against these URLs to measure:

  • Performance - How fast pages load for users (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Accessibility - How well pages work for users with disabilities, following WCAG guidelines (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • Best Practices - Whether pages follow modern web development standards (scores 0-100, higher is better)
  • SEO - How well pages are optimized for search engines (scores 0-100, higher is better)

Accessibility findings come from axe-core, the industry-standard accessibility testing engine embedded in Lighthouse. The axe findings surface specific WCAG violations such as missing alternative text, insufficient color contrast, and missing form labels that make government websites harder to use for people with disabilities.

Traffic data reflects daily visitor counts from DAP. URLs are ranked by page load count, ensuring the most-used government pages are prioritized for quality measurement.

Accessibility Trend Narrative#

Over the past 21 days of data (2026-02-26 to 2026-03-18), government website accessibility scores have improved (+24.66 points, from 68 to 92.66). Performance scores have improved (+17.32 points, from 38 to 55.32).

Today's aggregate accessibility score of 92.8 reflects the mean Lighthouse accessibility score across 100 successfully scanned government URLs. A score above 90 indicates generally strong compliance with WCAG automated checks, though manual testing is always recommended to fully assess accessibility.

Common Accessibility Issues (Top 10)#

Human impact narratives for top issues

The following narratives describe the real-world impact of each accessibility barrier on people with disabilities. These barriers are not abstract technical failures; they prevent citizens from independently accessing government services.

target-size: Digital Motor Access

Small touch targets act as a digital gatekeeper, excluding individuals with tremors, arthritis, or limited dexterity from accessing essential services independently. These technical failures transform a routine interaction into a source of failure, stripping away the autonomy of citizens who require a frictionless, accessible interface to participate in digital life. The approximately 58 million Americans with ambulatory or self-care disabilities are disproportionately impacted by inadequate touch target sizing on government mobile websites.

Affected groups:

  • People with Parkinson's disease, arthritis, or hand tremors
  • Older adults with reduced fine motor control
  • People with motor disabilities using alternative pointing devices
  • People in situational impairment contexts (e.g., commuting, holding a child)

heading-order: Document Structure Navigation

Screen reader users navigate complex government websites primarily through heading structure, using headings as a table of contents to jump between sections. Skipped heading levels break the logical document outline, causing confusion about the hierarchy of information and forcing users to re-read sections to understand the relationship between topics, adding significant time and effort to information-gathering tasks.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

landmark-one-main: Primary Content Identification

Without a main landmark, screen reader users lose their most efficient way to skip to the primary content of a government page. Every page visit requires tabbing through the entire navigation structure to reach the actual content, a significant time and effort burden that accumulates across repeated visits to government websites for citizens managing ongoing benefit cases or legal matters.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

color-contrast: Visual Information Access

Low contrast text is one of the most pervasive barriers on government websites, rendering critical information invisible to the approximately 26 million Americans with low vision or color blindness. When agency announcements, form instructions, error messages, or legal notices are displayed in insufficient contrast, affected citizens are denied equal access to the information they need to exercise their rights and access public services.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision including age-related vision loss
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

label-content-name-mismatch: Voice Control Activation

When the accessible name of an element does not match its visible text, voice control users cannot activate it using the words they see on screen. Saying "click Submit" to a button whose visual label is "Submit" but whose accessible name is "submitbtn" results in no action. This mismatch silently breaks voice control for citizens with motor disabilities who depend on this input method to interact with government websites.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People with low vision relying on screen magnifiers
  • People who are deaf using keyboard navigation
  • People with motor disabilities using voice control software

link-name: Link Purpose Clarity

Links without accessible names are completely useless to screen reader users who navigate government pages by jumping between links. An unnamed link could lead anywhere, and activating it unknowingly could trigger downloads, open unexpected pages, or initiate unintended processes. On government websites, unnamed links undermine the informed consent principle by preventing citizens from knowing where a link will take them.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities using keyboard navigation

image-alt: Visual Content Access

Images without alternative text are completely invisible to blind users and to search engines. On government websites, informational images, diagrams, infographics, and document screenshots without alt text deprive blind citizens of the same information available to sighted visitors. Critical details like emergency instructions, benefit eligibility charts, or form procedure diagrams can be entirely inaccessible, putting these citizens at a disadvantage in accessing government services and information.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools

link-in-text-block: Color-Independent Link Identification

When links are distinguished only by color, people with color vision deficiency cannot identify them as links and may miss critical references to additional information, forms, or resources. On government websites, overlooked links in informational content can mean missing essential instructions, deadlines, or procedures that affect a citizen's ability to access services or comply with requirements.

Affected groups:

  • People with low vision who may not perceive color differences
  • People who are color blind (approximately 8% of men, 0.5% of women)

aria-required-children: Widget Structure Integrity

ARIA composite widgets without the correct child roles create structurally broken interfaces for screen reader users. Menus without menu items, lists without list items, and grids without cells all degrade to incoherent structures, making navigation impossible and forcing blind users to abandon complex interactive government tools.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities navigating by keyboard

listitem: List Item Semantic Context

Orphaned list items outside of list containers lose their semantic meaning and list navigation properties. Screen reader users rely on list structure to understand that a set of items is related and to count the total number of items. Government navigation menus, step lists, and option groups presented as invalid lists are harder to parse and navigate efficiently.

Affected groups:

  • People who are blind using screen readers
  • People who are deaf and rely on visual-to-text tools
  • People with motor disabilities navigating by keyboard
Disability icon key
Without Vision — People who are blind or have no functional vision (1.0% of U.S. population — ~3,400,000 Americans)
Limited Vision — People with low vision who need magnification or high contrast (2.4% of U.S. population — ~8,100,000 Americans)
Without Perception of Color — People who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness) (4.3% of U.S. population — ~14,500,000 Americans)
Without Hearing — People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content (0.3% of U.S. population — ~1,100,000 Americans)
Limited Hearing — People with hearing loss who may struggle with audio without accommodations (3.5% of U.S. population — ~11,900,000 Americans)
Without Speech — People who cannot use speech or voice-based input effectively (0.5% of U.S. population — ~1,700,000 Americans)
Limited Manipulation — People with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity (2.2% of U.S. population — ~7,600,000 Americans)
Limited Reach and Strength — People with limited reach, strength, or stamina (5.8% of U.S. population — ~19,600,000 Americans)
Limited Language, Cognitive, and Learning Abilities — People with cognitive, learning, or language differences (4.7% of U.S. population — ~15,900,000 Americans)

These icons show which groups of people with disabilities are excluded by each accessibility barrier. Where page view data is available, each icon shows an estimated number of people potentially excluded (page loads for affected URLs × disability prevalence rate from U.S. Census ACS 2023). Hover over or focus an icon to see the full estimate and methodology. Icons follow the Section 508 Functional Performance Criteria and the equivalent EU EN 301 549 v3.2.1 Table B.2 categories.

Prevalence data: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (ACS) 2023 1-Year Estimates, Table B18101; supplemental data from CDC, NIDCD, AFB, and NIH/NEI. U.S. population base: ~336,500,000 (2023 estimate). Reviewed annually.

See the Section 508 WCAG to FPC mapping for additional detail on how accessibility requirements map to functional needs.

Usability Heuristics Summary#

The most common accessibility violations map to Nielsen Norman Group's 10 usability heuristics. This table shows which heuristics are most affected by today's top axe-core violations, helping to identify where systemic usability barriers exist.

NN/g usability heuristics affected by today's top accessibility violations

6 Recognition rather than recall 6 89 heading-order, landmark-one-main, color-contrast, link-name, aria-required-children, listitem

Estimated Impact (daily)#

No accessibility findings data available for this scan. The impact estimation requires detailed accessibility findings from scanning tools. Currently, the scanner is running in a mode that does not collect individual accessibility issues.

No accessibility findings data is available to estimate excluded users by disability category for this scan.

Performance Impact on Americans#

Google defines a good Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) as under 2.5 seconds and recommends pages under 1.6 MB. The figures below estimate how much extra time Americans spend waiting, and how much extra data is transferred, because government websites fall short of these benchmarks. Calculations are based on 96 successfully scanned URLs with Lighthouse timing data.

Estimated performance impact vs. Google benchmarks for government URLs scanned on March 19, 2026
Metric Estimated total (March 19, 2026) Notes
Extra time waiting (vs 2.5s LCP benchmark) 11 years, 4 months, 20 days 359,419,297 seconds
Extra data transferred (vs 1.6 MB page weight benchmark) ~64 TB (2,663 copies of Wikipedia) Across 96 URLs with weight data

Extra time is calculated as: for each scanned URL, max(0, actual LCP − 2.5s) × page loads. Extra data is calculated as: max(0, actual page weight − 1.6 MB) × page loads. LCP and page weight are measured by Lighthouse. Wikipedia copy count uses a size of 24.05 GB per Wikipedia:Size of Wikipedia. These are rough estimates based on a sample of the top government URLs by traffic.

https://federalregister.gov 145773 needs improvement 75 79 / 5 100 83
https://www.dol.gov 144259 poor 45 100 77 92
https://osha.gov 140622 poor 35 83 / 4 88 83
https://portal.cms.gov 133867 poor 56 100 69 82
https://tsaenrollmentbyidemia.tsa.dhs.gov 133046 poor 47 85 / 2 50 92
https://aviationweather.gov 131852 poor 80 92 / 2 100 100
https://search.usa.gov 130570 unknown 0 0 0 0
https://gsa.gov 130181 poor 35 100 / 1 92 92
https://about.usps.com 129657 poor 55 100 88 100
https://fbi.gov 127512 poor 43 79 / 3 77 54
https://osti.gov 126186 poor 79 96 / 1 96 100
https://ustraveldocs.com 126181 poor 49 90 / 1 92 91
https://army.mil 126141 unknown 0 0 0 0
https://history.state.gov 125221 poor 62 87 / 3 100 100
https://uspto.gov 123469 poor 53 88 / 3 77 92

Take Action#

Here is how you can help improve accessibility on U.S. government websites.

  • Read the federal accessibility report. The 2025 Governmentwide Section 508 Assessment from the GSA Administrator details the state of federal accessibility compliance and the steps agencies are taking to improve.
  • Submit URLs you care about. Want specific government pages included in future scans? Submit them to the Open Scans project to help broaden coverage.
  • Test with free tools. Do your own automated and manual accessibility testing with Accessibility Insights and Google Lighthouse. Automated tools catch a significant portion of WCAG failures quickly; manual review with assistive technologies catches the rest.
  • Adopt and update the U.S. Web Design System (USWDS). The USWDS is already a strong foundation for accessible, consistent federal websites, but many agencies have not adopted it or are running outdated versions. Encourage your agency to adopt or upgrade so every American benefits from its accessibility work.
  • Hire people with disabilities. People with disabilities are the ultimate experts on digital barriers. Bringing them into design, engineering, and testing teams — not just as consultants but as full-time employees — leads to more accessible products and services for everyone.

Accessibility Findings

https://tools.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 68

Axe findings: 10

`button`, `link`, and `menuitem` elements do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-command-name)

When an element doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make command elements more accessible.

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~52KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~52,749 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 2,197,890 affected page loads)

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Axe findings: 5

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~50KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~50,951 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads) ~91KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~91,287 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.one-sixth-first > div#forecast-lookup > form#getForecast > input#inputstring

Snippet:

<input id="inputstring" name="inputstring" type="text" value="Enter location ..." onclick="this.value=''" autocomplete="off">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~21KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~21,230 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads) ~6KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~6,369 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads) ~99KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~99,779 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~21KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~21,230 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads) ~6KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~6,369 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads)

Affected elements (3):

Element 1

Element path: div.div-full > div#wwamap > div.USMap > img#us

Snippet:

<img id="us" src="https://www.weather.gov/wwamap/png/US.png" usemap="#US_large_imagemap_800_500">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 2

Element path: div.div-full > div#wwamap > div.USMap > img#alaska

Snippet:

<img id="alaska" src="https://www.weather.gov/wwamap/png/ak.png" usemap="#alaska_imagemap_130_115">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Element 3

Element path: div.div-full > div#wwamap > div.USMap > img#hawaii

Snippet:

<img id="hawaii" src="https://www.weather.gov/wwamap/png/hi.png" usemap="#hawaii_imagemap">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~46KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~46,705 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads) ~123KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~123,131 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 2,122,953 affected page loads)

Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Accessibility Findings

https://informeddelivery.usps.com

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 71

Axe findings: 9

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

Disabilities affected: ~17KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~17,830 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,782,984 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~5,349 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 1,782,984 affected page loads) ~39KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~39,226 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 1,782,984 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#g-navigation > div.nav-full > nav > ul.nav-list

Snippet:

<ul class="nav-list" role="menubar">

Label: Skip Quick Tools Links Quick Tools Track a Package Informed Delivery …

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has children which are not allowed: li[tabindex]
`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent)

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Disabilities affected: ~17KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~17,830 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 1,782,984 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~39,226 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 1,782,984 affected page loads)

Affected elements (10):

Element 1

Element path: nav > ul.nav-list > li.qt-nav > a.nav-first-element

Snippet:

<a aria-expanded="false" role="menuitem" tabindex="0" aria-haspopup="true" class="nav-first-element menuitem" href="#">

Label: Quick Tools

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Required ARIA parents role not present: menu, menubar, group

Element 2

Element path: nav > ul.nav-list > li.menuheader > a#mail-ship-width

Snippet:

<a id="mail-ship-width" aria-expanded="false" class="menuitem" role="menuitem" tabindex="0" aria-haspopup="true" href="https://www.usps.com/ship/">

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Accessibility Findings

https://travel.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 93

Axe findings: 2

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~27KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~27,287 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 1,136,955 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0, maximum-scale=1.0, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~10KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Element path: div > div.blazy > div.media > img.media__element

Snippet:

<img decoding="async" class="media__element b-lazy" loading="lazy" src="" width="480" height="360">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 2.84 (foreground color: #999999, background color: #ffffff, font size: 8.0pt (10.6667px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Estimated ~42,953 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 913,899 affected page loads)

Accessibility Findings

https://cdc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 91

Axe findings: 3

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Link text (and alternate text for images, when used as links) that is discernible, unique, and focusable improves the navigation experience for screen reader users. Learn how to make links accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~8KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~8,511 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 851,058 affected page loads) ~2KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~2,553 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 851,058 affected page loads) ~18KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~18,723 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 851,058 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: nav.mobile-navbar > div.mobile-navbar_top > div.d-flex > a#cdc-mobile-search-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="cdc-mobile-search-toggle" class="nav-link" href="#" aria-controls="cdc-mobile-search-form">

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Element is in tab order and does not have accessible text
  • Fix any of the following:
  • Element does not have text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
Skip links are not focusable. (rule: skip-link)

Including a skip link can help users skip to the main content to save time. Learn more about skip links.

Label: Skip to search

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Estimated ~17,887 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 745,274 affected page loads) ~32KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`<td>` elements in a large `<table>` do not have table headers. (rule: td-has-header)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that `<td>` elements in a large table (3 or more cells in width and height) have an associated table header may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about table headers.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,798 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 479,765 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Elements with `role="dialog"` or `role="alertdialog"` do not have accessible names. (rule: aria-dialog-name)

ARIA dialog elements without accessible names may prevent screen readers users from discerning the purpose of these elements. Learn how to make ARIA dialog elements more accessible.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

https://urs.earthdata.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 81

Axe findings: 5

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~4KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~4,497 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 449,664 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~1,349 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 449,664 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#tophat3Widget > nav#tophat3WidgetNavBar > div.container-fluid > button#tophat3HamburgerButton

Snippet:

<button class="navbar-toggler" type="button" id="tophat3HamburgerButton">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 84

Axe findings: 6

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

ARIA input fields do not have accessible names (rule: aria-input-field-name)

When an input field doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it with a generic name, making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn more about input field labels.

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Element 1

Element path: div.usa-grid > div.usa-footer-logo > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.tsp.gov/contact/">

Label: contact us

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.5:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #d6d7d9, surrounding text: #aeb0b5)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text

Accessibility Findings

https://science.nasa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 95

Axe findings: 1

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,434 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 343,411 affected page loads) ~1KPeople who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Affected elements (4):

Element 1

Element path: header.hds-nasa-header > div.usa-nav-container > div.grid-col > button#global-navigation-trigger--mobile

<button id="global-navigation-trigger--mobile" class="hds-global-menu-toggle menu-toggle menu-mobile-toggle" aria-expanded="false" data-menu-action="open" data-menu-location="left">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>

  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Accessibility Findings

https://radar.weather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 77

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~3,253 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 325,280 affected page loads) ~976People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~976 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 325,280 affected page loads) ~15KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~15,288 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 325,280 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~7KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~7,807 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 325,280 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1, maximum-scale=1, user-scalable=0">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Accessibility Findings

https://my.uscis.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 89

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-*]` attributes do not match their roles (rule: aria-allowed-attr)

Each ARIA `role` supports a specific subset of `aria-*` attributes. Mismatching these invalidates the `aria-*` attributes. Learn how to match ARIA attributes to their roles.

Accessibility Findings

https://eeoc.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 2

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

https://catalog.archives.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 96

Axe findings: 2

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Disabilities affected: ~5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~5,482 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 249,168 affected page loads) ~14KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Estimated ~14,452 people potentially excluded (5.8% prevalence × 249,168 affected page loads)

Affected elements (4):

Element 1

Element path: div#nac-footer > div.flex-justify > div.display-flex > a.usa-link--white

Snippet:

<a data-testid="nac-header--help" class="usa-link--white font-sans-sm" href="/help/faq/login">

Label: Help with Login.gov

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (273.2px by 19.5px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 19.4px instead of at least 24px.

Element 2

Element path: div.flex-column > div.tablet:display-flex > div > a.usa-link--external

Snippet:

<a class="usa-link--external usa-link--white" href="https://www.archives.gov/contact" rel="noopener noreferrer" role="link" target="_blank">

Label: Contact Us

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (88.4px by 21.4px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 21.4px instead of at least 24px.

Element 3

Element path: div.flex-column > div.tablet:display-flex > div > a.usa-link--external

Snippet:

<a class="usa-link--external usa-link--white" href="https://www.archives.gov/global-pages/privacy.html" rel="noopener noreferrer" role="link" target="_blank">

Label: Privacy Policy

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (108.1px by 21.4px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20px instead of at least 24px.

Element 4

Element path: div.flex-column > div.tablet:display-flex > div > a.usa-link--external

Snippet:

<a class="usa-link--external usa-link--white" href="https://www.archives.gov/global-pages/accessibility" rel="noopener noreferrer" role="link" target="_blank">

Label: Accessibility

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (97.6px by 21.4px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 20px instead of at least 24px.
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,492 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 249,168 affected page loads) ~5KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~5,980 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 249,168 affected page loads) ~748People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~748 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 249,168 affected page loads) ~5KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~5,482 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 249,168 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.App > div#nac-footer > div.flex-justify > a.nac-logo

Snippet:

<a aria-label="NAC Home" class="nac-logo nac-logo--vertical theme-primary-dark height-full display-flex fl…" data-testid="nac-footer--logo" href="/">

Label: NATIONAL ARCHIVES CATALOG

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Text inside the element is not included in the accessible name

Accessibility Findings

https://esta.cbp.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 85

Axe findings: 4

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,439 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 243,925 affected page loads) ~732People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~732 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 243,925 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.toggle-language > span.dropdown > button#languageDrop > img

Snippet:

<img _ngcontent-ng-c799267185="" src="assets/images/flags/usUkFlag.png">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~5KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~5,854 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 243,925 affected page loads) ~10KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~10,489 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 243,925 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.col-xs-12 > ul > li#welcome.whoYouAre.1 > a.strong

Snippet:

<a tabindex="0" data-target="#visaModal" data-toggle="modal" class="strong esta-link" href="#">

Label: Visa Waiver Program

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.18:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #00416a, surrounding text: #333333)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,439 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 243,925 affected page loads) ~732People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~732 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 243,925 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.container > div.row > div.col-xs-12 > ul

Snippet:

<ul _ngcontent-ng-c2940030545="">

Label: About ESTA and The Visa Waiver Program What is the difference between an ESTA …

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • List element has direct children that are not allowed: div
List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~5,366 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 243,925 affected page loads)

Accessibility Findings

https://cms.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 90

Axe findings: 2

`[role]`s are not contained by their required parent element (rule: aria-required-parent)

Some ARIA child roles must be contained by specific parent roles to properly perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about ARIA roles and required parent element.

Estimated ~5,001 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 227,309 affected page loads)

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,273 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 227,309 affected page loads) ~682People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Accessibility Findings

https://fas.usda.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

`[aria-hidden="true"]` elements contain focusable descendents (rule: aria-hidden-focus)

Focusable descendents within an `[aria-hidden="true"]` element prevent those interactive elements from being available to users of assistive technologies like screen readers. Learn how `aria-hidden` affects focusable elements.

Disabilities affected: ~2KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~2,264 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 226,367 affected page loads) ~5KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~5,433 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 226,367 affected page loads) ~679People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~679 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 226,367 affected page loads) ~4KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~4,980 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 226,367 affected page loads)

Label: Why Do Ag Exports Matter to U.S. Farmers and the U.S. Economy? Learn More

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Focusable content should have tabindex="-1" or be removed from the DOM

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Accessibility Findings

https://dea.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 89

Axe findings: 4

`<frame>` or `<iframe>` elements do not have a title (rule: frame-title)

Screen reader users rely on frame titles to describe the contents of frames. Learn more about frame titles.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Some elements have a `[tabindex]` value greater than 0 (rule: tabindex)

A value greater than 0 implies an explicit navigation ordering. Although technically valid, this often creates frustrating experiences for users who rely on assistive technologies. Learn more about the `tabindex` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Accessibility Findings

https://sam.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 97

Axe findings: 2

Elements use prohibited ARIA attributes (rule: aria-prohibited-attr)

Using ARIA attributes in roles where they are prohibited can mean that important information is not communicated to users of assistive technologies. Learn more about prohibited ARIA roles.

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.paragraph__column > div.px-video-container > div#progress-bar > div#progress

Snippet:

<div class="progress" id="progress" aria-label="Video: " style="width: 0%;">

Label: Video:

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • aria-label attribute cannot be used on a div with no valid role attribute.
Elements with visible text labels do not have matching accessible names. (rule: label-content-name-mismatch)

Visible text labels that do not match the accessible name can result in a confusing experience for screen reader users. Learn more about accessible names.

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Estimated ~4,595 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 191,464 affected page loads) ~8KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

Accessibility Findings

https://earthquake.usgs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 94

Axe findings: 2

`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,797 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 179,665 affected page loads) ~539People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~539 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 179,665 affected page loads) ~8KPeople with cognitive, learning, or language differences. Estimated ~8,444 people potentially excluded (4.7% prevalence × 179,665 affected page loads)

Element 1

Element path: html

Snippet:

<html>

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The <html> element does not have a lang attribute

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,871 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 161,302 affected page loads) ~6KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Label: Regional English Language Officers (RELOs)

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 1.98:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #1c2b58, surrounding text: #595a5c)

https://nhtsa.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 85

Axe findings: 6

`[aria-*]` attributes do not have valid values (rule: aria-valid-attr-value)

Assistive technologies, like screen readers, can't interpret ARIA attributes with invalid values. Learn more about valid values for ARIA attributes.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,580 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 157,951 affected page loads) ~474People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~474 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 157,951 affected page loads) ~3KPeople with limited hand, finger, or fine motor dexterity. Estimated ~3,475 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 157,951 affected page loads)

Label: Vehicle

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Snippet:

<input type="submit" value="Search">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 4.11 (foreground color: #ffffff, background color: #357ae8, font size: 10.0pt (13.3333px), font weight: bold). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
`<html>` element does not have a `[lang]` attribute (rule: html-has-lang)

If a page doesn't specify a `lang` attribute, a screen reader assumes that the page is in the default language that the user chose when setting up the screen reader. If the page isn't actually in the default language, then the screen reader might not announce the page's text correctly. Learn more about the `lang` attribute.

Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Tables do not use `<caption>` instead of cells with the `[colspan]` attribute to indicate a caption. (rule: table-fake-caption)

Screen readers have features to make navigating tables easier. Ensuring that tables use the actual caption element instead of cells with the `[colspan]` attribute may improve the experience for screen reader users. Learn more about captions.

Accessibility Findings

https://osha.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 83

Axe findings: 4

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,406 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 140,622 affected page loads) ~422People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div#hero-callout > p > a > img.align-center

Snippet:

<img aalt="OSHA Cares" src="/sites/default/files/inline-images/OSHA-Cares_logo.png" style="margin:0 auto; display: block; object-fit: initial;" width="400" class="align-center">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.

Touch targets do not have sufficient size or spacing. (rule: target-size)

Touch targets with sufficient size and spacing help users who may have difficulty targeting small controls to activate the targets. Learn more about touch targets.

Estimated ~3,094 people potentially excluded (2.2% prevalence × 140,622 affected page loads) ~8KPeople with limited reach, strength, or stamina. Affected elements (2):

Element 1

Element path: div.field > div.address > p.address-block > a

Snippet:

<a href="tel:800-321-6742" onmousedown="_
                                                sendEvent('Telephone Cli…" target="_self">

Label: 1-800-321-6742

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (96px by 19px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 21.2px instead of at least 24px.

Element 2

Element path: div.field > div.address > p.address-block > a

Snippet:

<a href="https://www.osha.gov" target="_self">

Label: www.osha.gov

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (91.6px by 19px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 21.2px instead of at least 24px.

Accessibility Findings

https://tsaenrollmentbyidemia.tsa.dhs.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 85

Axe findings: 4

Image elements do not have `[alt]` attributes (rule: image-alt)

Informative elements should aim for short, descriptive alternate text. Decorative elements can be ignored with an empty alt attribute. Learn more about the `alt` attribute.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,330 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 133,046 affected page loads) ~399People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~399 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 133,046 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.col-12 > div.official-banner > div#accordion > img.ues-usa-img

Snippet:

<img src="/images/us_flag_small.png" class="ues-usa-img">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have an alt attribute
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Links rely on color to be distinguishable. (rule: link-in-text-block)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Link text that is discernible improves the experience for users with low vision. Learn how to make links distinguishable.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,193 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 133,046 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~5,721 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 133,046 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.col-12 > div.official-banner > div#accordion > a#banner-toggle

Snippet:

<a id="banner-toggle" data-toggle="collapse" href="#" data-target="#collapseOne" aria-expanded="true">

Label: Here's how you know.

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • The link has insufficient color contrast of 2.01:1 with the surrounding text. (Minimum contrast is 3:1, link text: #0f68b8, surrounding text: #373a3c)
  • The link has no styling (such as underline) to distinguish it from the surrounding text
`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,193 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 133,046 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: head > meta

Snippet:

<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, user-scalable=no">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices
Document does not have a main landmark. (rule: landmark-one-main)

One main landmark helps screen reader users navigate a web page. Learn more about landmarks.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,330 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 133,046 affected page loads) ~399People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Affected elements (1):

Element path: html.no-js

Snippet:

How to fix: Fix all of the following:

  • Document does not have a main landmark

Accessibility Findings

https://aviationweather.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 92

Axe findings: 3

Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,164 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 131,852 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~5,670 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 131,852 affected page loads)

Snippet:

<h4>

Snippet:

<span>

Label: Legend

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

Element 4

Element path: footer#footer-navbar > div.container-fluid > a.navbar-brand > span#rev

Snippet:

<span id="rev">

Label: v4.13

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Links do not have a discernible name (rule: link-name)

Label: Slide 3

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

Element 4

Element path: section.hero-section > div.hero-indicators > div.d-flex > button.indicator

Snippet:

<button class="indicator" aria-label="Slide 4" data-slide="4">

Label: Slide 4

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Target has insufficient size (12px by 12px, should be at least 24px by 24px)
  • Target has insufficient space to its closest neighbors. Safe clickable space has a diameter of 16px instead of at least 24px.

Heading elements are not in a sequentially-descending order (rule: heading-order)

Properly ordered headings that do not skip levels convey the semantic structure of the page, making it easier to navigate and understand when using assistive technologies. Learn more about heading order.

`[user-scalable="no"]` is used in the `<meta name="viewport">` element or the `[maximum-scale]` attribute is less than 5. (rule: meta-viewport)

Disabling zooming is problematic for users with low vision who rely on screen magnification to properly see the contents of a web page. Learn more about the viewport meta tag.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,028 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 126,181 affected page loads)

<meta name="viewport" content="user-scalable=no,initial-scale=1,maximum-scale=1,minimum-scale=1,width=dev…">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • user-scalable=no on <meta> tag disables zooming on mobile devices

Accessibility Findings

https://history.state.gov

Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 87

Axe findings: 3

Buttons do not have an accessible name (rule: button-name)

When a button doesn't have an accessible name, screen readers announce it as "button", making it unusable for users who rely on screen readers. Learn how to make buttons more accessible.

Disabilities affected: ~1KPeople who are blind or have no functional vision. Estimated ~1,252 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 125,221 affected page loads) ~376People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Estimated ~376 people potentially excluded (0.3% prevalence × 125,221 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.searchbox > form#navigationSearchForm > p.form-group > button.hsg-link-button

Snippet:

<button type="submit" class="hsg-link-button search-button btn">

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  • aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  • aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  • Element has no title attribute
  • Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  • Element does not have an explicit <label>
  • Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"
Background and foreground colors do not have a sufficient contrast ratio. (rule: color-contrast)

Low-contrast text is difficult or impossible for many users to read. Learn how to provide sufficient color contrast.

Disabilities affected: ~3KPeople with low vision who need magnification or high contrast. Estimated ~3,005 people potentially excluded (2.4% prevalence × 125,221 affected page loads) ~5KPeople who cannot distinguish certain colors (color blindness). Estimated ~5,385 people potentially excluded (4.3% prevalence × 125,221 affected page loads)

Affected elements (1):

Element 1

Element path: div.container > div.row > p > a

Snippet:

<a href="mailto:history@state.gov?subject=Error%20on%20page%20%60%2Fexist%2Fapps%2F…">

Label: Report an issue on this page

How to fix: Fix any of the following:

  • Element has insufficient color contrast of 3.51 (foreground color: #205493, background color: #aeb0b5, font size: 9.8pt (13px), font weight: normal). Expected contrast ratio of 4.5:1
Lists do not contain only `<li>` elements and script supporting elements (`<script>` and `<template>`). (rule: list)

Screen readers have a specific way of announcing lists. Ensuring proper list structure aids screen reader output. Learn more about proper list structure.

Estimated ~1,252 people potentially excluded (1.0% prevalence × 125,221 affected page loads) ~376People who are deaf and cannot hear audio content. Lighthouse Accessibility Score: 88

Axe findings: 3

Elements with an ARIA `[role]` that require children to contain a specific `[role]` are missing some or all of those required children. (rule: aria-required-children)

Some ARIA parent roles must contain specific child roles to perform their intended accessibility functions. Learn more about roles and required children elements.

List items (`<li>`) are not contained within `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` parent elements. (rule: listitem)

Screen readers require list items (`<li>`) to be contained within a parent `<ul>`, `<ol>` or `<menu>` to be announced properly. Learn more about proper list structure.
